title: Run one or more clustering methods on a CSV and write per-row memberships
summary: Loads a CSV, applies a user-specified list of method blocks (each over a range of k), and writes a single memberships.csv with one column per produced solution. Ensemble blocks run in a second pass so they can reference any other solution from the same call.
description:
`segment` is the core command of the clustering plugin. It reads one CSV, prepares the feature matrix (applies the NA policy, drops rows with non-finite values when `na_action = "complete_cases"`), then runs each entry of `methods` in order. Non-ensemble blocks run first; ensemble blocks run in a second pass so they can consume any solution by name via `depends_on`.

Every method block produces one solution column per k value, named `<run_name>_<method>_k<k>`. All columns are written to a single `memberships.csv` aligned to the UUID column.
usage:
[["clustering::segment"]]
input = "<csv-path>"
uuid_col = "<id-column>"
vars = ["<feature1>", "<feature2>", ...]
run_dir = "<output-root>"
run_name = "<run-id>"
random_seed = <int>
methods = [
  { method = "kmeans", k_range = [2, 5] },
  { method = "hier",   k_range = [2, 5], hier_method = "ward.D2" },
]

arguments.name: input
arguments.description: Path to a CSV with a header row (unless headers = false).

arguments.name: uuid_col
arguments.description: Column containing a per-row unique identifier. Memberships join back to this column.

arguments.name: vars
arguments.description: Feature column names to cluster on. Must all be numeric (or integer-coded categorical for latent_class).

arguments.name: run_dir
arguments.description: Root output directory. A `<run_name>_clustering/` subdirectory is created under this path.

arguments.name: run_name
arguments.description: Short identifier for this run; prefixes every output solution column name.

arguments.name: methods
arguments.description: Array of method blocks; see the methods section for supported entries.

arguments.name: na_action
arguments.description: complete_cases drops rows with any non-finite feature; fail aborts if any such row is present.

arguments.name: headers
arguments.description: Whether the CSV has a header row.

arguments.name: random_seed
arguments.description: Base seed. A per-(method, k) seed is derived from this so each method is deterministic.

details:
## Method blocks

Each entry in `methods` is a small table with a `method` key plus any method-specific knobs. k-bearing methods accept either `k_range = [lo, hi]` (inclusive) or an explicit `k_list = [k1, k2, ...]`; exactly one of the two is required. HDBSCAN uses `min_pts_range` / `min_pts_list` instead. Distance-based methods accept a `metric` knob (euclidean default; manhattan, maximum, canberra, minkowski supported where relevant). Pass `random_seed` at the top level and every method derives its own per-(method, k) seed from it.

Every block also accepts `threads` (integer ≥ 1, default 1). It parallelises random restarts for `kmeans`, `archetypes`, `gmm`, `latent_class`, and `spectral`; other methods ignore it. Results are deterministic regardless of thread count when `random_seed` is set.

## Choosing a method

- **Compact, roughly spherical clusters in Euclidean space** — start with `kmeans`. Fast and the baseline everything else is compared against.
- **Outliers or heavy-tailed features, want medoids not means** — `kmedoids` (PAM). Slower but robust; pairs well with `metric = "manhattan"`.
- **You want a dendrogram and interpretable merges** — `hier` with `ward.D2`. A good exploratory tool before committing to a k.
- **A few well-separated groups that agglomerative linkage might under-split** — `diana`. Divisive is often cleaner top-down.
- **Wide feature matrix with correlated columns** — `pca_to_hier` to denoise first, then cluster in the principal-component space.
- **You like hierarchical structure but want a final partitional polish** — `hier_to_kmeans` (Ward seed → Lloyd).
- **Soft / overlapping membership is meaningful (e.g. personas)** — `fuzzy` c-means.
- **Non-convex clusters, manifold shapes, graph-like structure** — `spectral`.
- **Density-defined clusters with explicit noise flagging** — `hdbscan`. Don't pre-specify k; sweep `min_pts_range`.
- **You want extremal exemplars, not central prototypes** — `archetypes`. Rows become convex combinations of archetype corners.
- **Exemplar-based with automatic k pressure** — `affinity` propagation.
- **Manifest variables are categorical (survey items, Likert codes)** — `latent_class`. EM on multinomial emissions — unsupervised naive Bayes.
- **Continuous features, parametric probabilistic model, soft posteriors** — `gmm`. Pick `diagonal` (default, fast, axis-aligned), `full` (per-component shape), or `tied` (shared Σ, the unsupervised-LDA analogue — posterior gate becomes a softmax of linear discriminant scores).
- **Multiple base methods disagree and you want a stable consensus** — `ensemble`.

## Method reference

### kmeans
**How it works.** Lloyd's algorithm with k-means++ initialisation. Minimises the within-cluster sum of squared Euclidean distances to centroids.

**Choose when.** Features are continuous, roughly on the same scale, and you expect compact isotropic clusters. The default baseline — run it first to see what a geometry-only fit looks like.

**Knobs.** `k_range` / `k_list`, `max_iter`, `nrep` (best of several random starts).

**Caveats.** Sensitive to scale and outliers — standardise features first. Struggles with elongated, non-convex, or varying-density clusters.

### kmedoids
**How it works.** Partitioning Around Medoids (build + swap). Each cluster is represented by an actual data row (the medoid), minimising total dissimilarity.

**Choose when.** You want robustness to outliers, or a non-Euclidean metric (manhattan, minkowski). Medoid rows are often easier to interpret than k-means centroids because they are real respondents.

**Knobs.** `k_range` / `k_list`, `metric`.

**Caveats.** O(n² k) swap phase — slower than k-means on larger n.

### hier
**How it works.** Agglomerative bottom-up linkage on a pairwise distance matrix; the dendrogram is cut at the requested k.

**Choose when.** You want to inspect the merge hierarchy, have small-to-medium n (≤ a few thousand rows), or expect a ward.D2-friendly variance structure.

**Knobs.** `k_range` / `k_list`, `hier_method` ∈ {single, complete, average, ward.D, ward.D2, median, centroid, mcquitty}, `metric`.

**Caveats.** O(n²) memory. Single-linkage chains; ward requires squared-Euclidean geometry.

### diana
**How it works.** Divisive hierarchical clustering (Kaufman-Rousseeuw). Starts from one cluster containing every point and recursively splits the most dissimilar subgroup.

**Choose when.** You suspect a few large, well-separated top-level groups that agglomerative linkage might under-split.

**Knobs.** `k_range` / `k_list`, `metric`.

**Caveats.** O(n²) and slower than `hier` — practical only at modest n.

### hier_to_kmeans
**How it works.** Run hierarchical linkage, cut at k to get initial assignments, then re-run Lloyd from the resulting centroids.

**Choose when.** You want the stability of hierarchical seeding with a final k-means polish — often tighter than either alone.

**Knobs.** All `hier` knobs plus k-means `max_iter`.

**Caveats.** Inherits hierarchical's O(n²) memory cost.

### pca_to_hier
**How it works.** Reduce the feature matrix to `ncp` principal components, then run hierarchical linkage in the reduced space.

**Choose when.** You have many correlated numeric features and want to denoise or sphere the geometry before clustering.

**Knobs.** `ncp`, `hier_method`, `metric`.

**Caveats.** PCA is unsupervised — directions of highest variance are not always the cluster-discriminating directions. Check cumulative variance explained before choosing `ncp`.

### fuzzy
**How it works.** Fuzzy c-means. Minimises a weighted sum of squared distances where each row holds a continuous membership vector over clusters. Hard assignments are the argmax of the membership matrix.

**Choose when.** Boundaries between clusters are genuinely soft (persona overlap, graded segments) and the fractional memberships themselves are useful.

**Knobs.** `k_range` / `k_list`, `m` (fuzzifier; default 2.0 — larger = softer), `max_iter`.

**Caveats.** Still driven by Euclidean distance to centroids — same convex-cluster assumption as k-means.

### spectral
**How it works.** Build an RBF-kernel similarity graph, compute the leading k eigenvectors of the normalised Laplacian, then run k-means in the embedding.

**Choose when.** Clusters are non-convex — concentric rings, half-moons, manifolds. Also the natural choice when your data is already graph-structured.

**Knobs.** `k_range` / `k_list`, `sigma` (kernel bandwidth).

**Caveats.** Quadratic in n for the affinity matrix; bandwidth tuning matters a lot — test a range of `sigma`.

### hdbscan
**How it works.** Hierarchical density-based clustering: build a mutual-reachability tree and extract the flat partition that maximises cluster persistence. Low-density rows are labelled noise.

**Choose when.** You don't know k, clusters have variable density, and you want an explicit noise / outlier label. Good for anomaly-adjacent analyses.

**Knobs.** `min_pts_range` / `min_pts_list`, `noise_policy` (`own_cluster` puts noise in cluster 0; `drop` removes noise rows from the memberships output).

**Caveats.** Not the right tool when every row must belong to a "real" cluster — `own_cluster` papers over the fact that some rows are genuinely noise.

### archetypes
**How it works.** Cutler-Breiman archetypal analysis: find k extremal "archetype" points (convex combinations of data rows) and represent each row as a convex combination of archetypes. Alternating projected-gradient on simplex-constrained least squares.

**Choose when.** You want corner / exemplar profiles rather than central prototypes — e.g. "most price-sensitive," "most quality-focused" — with rows interpreted as blends of those poles.

**Knobs.** `k_range` / `k_list`, `nrep` (restarts, best RSS wins), `maxit`, `inner_iters`.

**Caveats.** Hard assignments are the argmax of archetype weights, but the fractional weights are often the real interpretable output.

### affinity
**How it works.** Affinity propagation (Frey & Dueck) — message-passing on similarities selects exemplars. Wrapped here in a preference-bisection loop that targets a specific K.

**Choose when.** You want exemplar-based clusters with a target cluster count in mind, and the exemplar interpretation matters.

**Knobs.** `k_range` / `k_list`, `r` (neg-distance exponent), `prc` (bisection tolerance), `q` (damping).

**Caveats.** Slow at larger n; the bisection adds overhead on top of AP's per-iteration cost.

### latent_class
**How it works.** Categorical latent-class analysis via EM with a log-sum-exp E-step. Emissions are independent multinomials per manifest variable given latent class — the unsupervised-naive-Bayes analogue.

**Choose when.** Your `vars` are categorical / ordinal integer codes (survey items, Likert, multiple choice). Posterior class probabilities fall out of the fit as a byproduct.

**Knobs.** `k_range` / `k_list`, `nrep`, `maxiter`, `tol`.

**Caveats.** Non-integer floats are rounded to the nearest integer. Constant columns are dropped — need ≥ 2 active manifest variables. Local optima and label-switching are real — keep `nrep` ≥ 10.

### gmm
**How it works.** Gaussian mixture model fit by EM with a k-means warm-start for the component means. Three covariance parameterisations:

- `diagonal` (default) — per-component variance vector. Fastest; assumes axis-aligned clusters.
- `full` — per-component p×p covariance via Cholesky. Richest shape model; most parameters.
- `tied` — a single p×p covariance shared across all components. This is the **unsupervised-LDA analogue**: with shared Σ the posterior gate collapses to a softmax of linear discriminant scores δ_k(x) = xᵀΣ⁻¹μ_k − ½ μ_kᵀΣ⁻¹μ_k + log π_k. Aliases: `tied`, `shared`, `lda`.

**Choose when.** Continuous features, you want a parametric probabilistic model with soft posteriors. Pick `tied` when you believe clusters share shape but differ in location (the classical LDA assumption); `full` when shapes genuinely differ; `diagonal` when you want cheap and axis-aligned.

**Knobs.** `k_range` / `k_list`, `covariance`, `nrep`, `km_iter` (warm-up k-means iterations), `em_iter`, `tol`, `var_floor`.

**Caveats.** EM finds local optima — raise `nrep`. Full covariance costs O(k p²) parameters and can overfit on short / wide data; tied is a parsimonious middle ground between diagonal and full.

### ensemble
**How it works.** Consensus clustering via the co-association matrix: count how often each pair of rows co-clusters across the base solutions listed in `depends_on`, then cluster the co-association matrix with either hierarchical linkage or PAM.

**Choose when.** Multiple base methods give plausible-but-different answers and you want a stable synthesis, or you want to check solution stability before committing to one partitioner.

**Knobs.** `k_range` / `k_list`, `engine` (`hier` default, or `pam`), `depends_on` (fully-qualified prior-solution names from the same `segment` call).

**Caveats.** Cannot depend on other ensemble solutions — only primary base solutions. Ensemble blocks run in a second pass so block order within `methods` does not matter.

## Derived solution names
Each produced solution column is named `<run_name>_<method>_k<k>`. For example, `run_name = "p1"` with `method = "kmeans", k_range = [2, 3]` produces two columns: `p1_kmeans_k2` and `p1_kmeans_k3`. HDBSCAN uses `<run_name>_hdbscan_mp<min_pts>` instead of `_k<k>`.

## Determinism
Pass `random_seed` for reproducible output. Each method derives its own seed from a hash of the base seed, method name, and k so repeating a run reproduces every column exactly.
value:
On success, the command writes:

- `<run_dir>/ocinfo/<run_name>_clustering/memberships.csv` — UUIDs plus one column per solution.

And emits protocol events: `event.run.started`, zero or more `event.warning` (per-block errors that did not abort the run), `event.run.finished` with the memberships artifact and a summary.
notes: Latent class expects integer-valued manifest variables; non-integer floats are rounded to the nearest integer.
An individual method-block error is captured as a warning and the run continues with the remaining blocks.
Ensemble blocks cannot depend on other ensemble blocks — only primary solutions.
HDBSCAN is the only method using `min_pts_*` instead of `k_*`.

see_also.description: Score the solutions produced by segment against the original features.

examples.title: k-means over k=2..5
examples.body:
[["clustering::segment"]]
input = "./data/respondents.csv"
uuid_col = "respondent_id"
vars = ["q1", "q2", "q3", "q4"]
run_dir = "./runs"
run_name = "kmeans_demo"
random_seed = 42
methods = [
  { method = "kmeans", k_range = [2, 5] },
]

examples.title: Consensus ensemble
examples.body:
[["clustering::segment"]]
input = "./data/respondents.csv"
uuid_col = "respondent_id"
vars = ["q1", "q2", "q3", "q4"]
run_dir = "./runs"
run_name = "ens"
random_seed = 42
methods = [
  { method = "kmeans",   k_range = [3, 3] },
  { method = "hier",     k_range = [3, 3], hier_method = "ward.D2" },
  { method = "kmedoids", k_range = [3, 3] },
  { method = "ensemble", k_range = [3, 3], engine = "pam", depends_on = ["ens_kmeans_k3", "ens_hier_k3", "ens_kmedoids_k3"] },
]

examples.title: Gaussian mixture model
examples.body:
[["clustering::segment"]]
input = "./data/respondents.csv"
uuid_col = "respondent_id"
vars = ["pref_quality", "pref_price", "pref_brand", "pref_speed"]
run_dir = "./runs"
run_name = "gmm_demo"
random_seed = 42
methods = [
  { method = "gmm", k_range = [2, 5], covariance = "full", nrep = 5 },
]

examples.title: Unsupervised LDA (tied-covariance GMM)
examples.body:
[["clustering::segment"]]
input = "./data/respondents.csv"
uuid_col = "respondent_id"
vars = ["pref_quality", "pref_price", "pref_brand", "pref_speed"]
run_dir = "./runs"
run_name = "ulda_demo"
random_seed = 42
methods = [
  { method = "gmm", k_range = [2, 5], covariance = "tied", nrep = 5 },
]

examples.title: Latent-class on survey items
examples.body:
[["clustering::segment"]]
input = "./data/survey_items.csv"
uuid_col = "respondent_id"
vars = ["item_q1", "item_q2", "item_q3", "item_q4", "item_q5"]
run_dir = "./runs"
run_name = "lca"
random_seed = 2026
methods = [
  { method = "latent_class", k_range = [2, 5], nrep = 20, maxiter = 5000 },
]

fields.name: methods
fields.description: Array of method blocks to run.

fields.name: headers
fields.description: Whether the CSV has a header row.

fields.name: random_seed
fields.description: Optional base seed for deterministic output.
